dominant_error_pattern: Student inverts hemispheric specialization, attributing novel nonverbal problem-solving tasks primarily to left-hemisphere dominance rather than right-hemisphere function.
instructional_red_flags: Use a side-by-side hemispheric chart: left = language, sequential, familiar patterns; right = spatial, novel, relational processing. Present real cognitive tasks: (1) Define a word [left], (2) Solve a novel maze [right], (3) Matrix Reasoning [right]. Ask student to localize each; use neuroimaging or functional diagrams to validate right-hemisphere primacy for novel problem-solving as a final check.

dominant_error_pattern: Student collapses the intentional manipulation of phonemes (e.g., "say cat without /c/") with simpler word-discrimination or whole-word memory tasks, missing phonemic awareness's active, transformative demand.
instructional_red_flags: Teach via explicit task hierarchy: (Level 1) Discrimination: Do BAT and BAG start the same? (Level 2) Segmentation: Break CAT into sounds. (Level 3) Deletion/Substitution: Say CAT without /c/; say CAT and change /t/ to /p/. Use CTOPP-2 item examples and Lindamood curriculum models to anchor Level 3 as phonemic awareness. Use student-generated examples of each level to verify mastery.

dominant_error_pattern: Student re-prescribes dyslexia screening when reading evaluation is already active and quantitative-concept weakness emerges, overlooking the signal for dyscalculia as a second, distinct learning-disorder domain.
instructional_red_flags: Walk through Deb's referral sequentially: Step 1 = Reading evaluation triggered; Step 2 = New finding (quantitative weakness on WJ-IV). Ask: "What domain is under-evaluated?" Teach DSM-5 learning-disorder specificity: reading disorder, mathematics disorder, and disorder of written expression are distinct pathways, each requiring separate assessment. Use a decision tree to show when to add or expand screening pathways.
